title: 1. Background and History of Cosmetics in TCM
text:
The history of cosmetics in TCM is as long as the history of TCM itself. China’s oldest
medical text, Huangdi Neijing (黃帝內經), records foods and herbs for cosmetic purposes and
elaborates on our aging process was as well. Shennong Bencaojing (神農本草經: c. A.D. 200)
introduced over 30 herbs for cosmetic uses. Sun Simiao introduced cosmetic acupuncture
and more than 100 herbs for cosmetic purposes in his book Qianjin Yaofang (千金要方: c. A.D.
700). Heo Jun in Korea even wrote a 'cosmetic mask pack' made of herbs, along with other
various cosmetic interventions in his book, Dongui Bogam (東醫寶鑑: A.D. 1600). Korean
cosmetic mask packs are gaining popularity even in the West nowadays.
Considering nature of TCM, this kind of development of cosmetic treatment is very
natural. TCM does not differentiate between cosmetic treatment and ailment treatment. They
are divided only by purpose. A change of complexion in the face is an important tool of
diagnosis  for  internal  organs.  The  TCM  term,  Gwanhyungchalsaek  (觀形察色)  means
'observing the ﬁgure and color of the face'. Therefore, restoring the complexion of face right
is a part of treatment. On the other hand, healing the internal organs is a part of cosmetic
treatment. In conclusion, cosmetic treatment and regular treatment are actually one thing.
